participants.extracted: Twenty-eight patients with a clinical diagnosis of tuberous sclerosis complex were enrolled from the Tuberous Sclerosis Center of Excellence at the University of Texas Medical School at Houston between January and August 2010. All were older than 13 years (mean age 23 years); 15 were men and 13 were women. The sample was racially/ethnically diverse, including 72% non-Hispanic White, 7% Hispanic, 10% African American, 7% Asian, and 4% Native American participants. Exclusion criteria included pregnancy, current oral rapamycin use, and any immune dysfunction. Of the 28 enrolled, 23 completed the 6-month study; withdrawals were due to burning sensation with application, poor compliance, and one prolonged hospitalization unrelated to the study product.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to apply one of three topical study products nightly for 6 months: placebo vehicle with no rapamycin, low-dose topical rapamycin 0.003% (1 mg per 30 cc) compounded with Skincerity®, or high-dose topical rapamycin 0.015% (5 mg per 30 cc) compounded with Skincerity®. The product was applied as a thin coating directly over the facial treatment area, allowed to air dry for 60 minutes, and washed off gently the following morning.

outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: safety of topical rapamycin, assessed by serum rapamycin concentration (systemic absorption) measured monthly during the 6-month treatment period, with complete blood counts (white blood cell count, hemoglobin, platelet count) monitored monthly for evidence of systemic effects. Secondary outcome: efficacy of topical rapamycin for facial angiofibromas, assessed at study completion after 6 months by participant self-report of overall lesion appearance using the categories “got better on the treatment,” “got worse on the treatment,” or “the treatment made no difference.”
